title: Evolution & Animals
whatItIs:
Evolution by natural selection is the central organising principle of biology — the mechanism by which life has diversified from single-celled organisms into the millions of species that inhabit Earth today. Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace independently arrived at the insight that organisms vary, that variation is heritable, and that those individuals whose variations make them better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce more successfully. Over geological time, this process produces adaptation, speciation, and the branching tree of life that connects every living thing on Earth through shared ancestry.

The animal kingdom is one of the most spectacular products of this process. From the Cambrian Explosion roughly 540 million years ago — when most major animal body plans appeared in a geologically brief burst — animals have diversified into an extraordinary range of forms. Insects alone account for more species than all other animals combined. Marine ecosystems harbour everything from microscopic plankton to the blue whale, the largest animal ever to have lived. Animal intelligence spans a remarkable spectrum: octopuses solve puzzles and use tools despite having evolved complex cognition independently from vertebrates, corvids demonstrate planning and social reasoning, elephants mourn their dead, and cetaceans have culture — distinct learned behaviours passed between generations.

Human evolution is part of this story, not separate from it. Homo sapiens emerged in Africa and spread across the globe, coexisting for tens of thousands of years with other human species including Neanderthals and Denisovans (with whom we interbred — most people of non-African descent carry Neanderthal DNA today). Understanding our evolutionary heritage explains everything from why we crave sugar and fat, to why we form tribal in-groups, to why we are capable of both extraordinary cooperation and extraordinary cruelty.
whyItMatters:
Evolution is not just an academic subject — it has profound practical consequences. Modern medicine depends on evolutionary thinking: antibiotic resistance is evolution in real time, and understanding viral evolution is essential for pandemic preparedness. Agriculture relies on understanding selective breeding, genetic diversity, and co-evolution between crops and their pollinators and pests. Conservation biology is fundamentally about understanding how species adapt (or fail to adapt) to changing environments, and the current mass extinction event — driven primarily by habitat destruction, climate change, and human activity — is eliminating species faster than at any point since the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs.

Studying animals also challenges human exceptionalism in productive ways. The more we learn about animal cognition, communication, and social behaviour, the harder it becomes to draw a sharp line between 'us' and 'them.' Great apes use sign language and understand syntax. Dolphins have individual names. Crows remember human faces and teach their offspring which humans are dangerous. These discoveries raise ethical questions about how we treat other species — from factory farming to habitat destruction to the use of animals in research — and force us to reconsider what makes human consciousness special, if anything.
keyIdeas: Natural selection — variation, inheritance, and differential reproductive success
Adaptation and the mechanisms of evolutionary change
Speciation — how one species becomes two through isolation and divergence
Mass extinction events and their role in reshaping life on Earth
Animal intelligence — cognition, tool use, and culture across species
Human evolution — our place in the primate family tree and what makes us distinct
frontier: Genomic sequencing is revolutionising our understanding of evolutionary relationships and processes. Ancient DNA extracted from fossils is rewriting the story of human migration and revealing interbreeding between human species that was previously unknown. CRISPR gene editing raises the possibility of directing evolution deliberately — including controversial proposals for de-extinction of species like the woolly mammoth. Meanwhile, the study of convergent evolution (where unrelated species independently evolve similar solutions to the same problems) is revealing deep principles about which forms and strategies evolution 'discovers' repeatedly.
openQuestions: Is the evolution of complex intelligence an inevitable outcome given enough time, or a rare accident?
How should we define animal consciousness, and which species possess it?
Can we reverse the current mass extinction, or are we past the point of no return for many ecosystems?
What role does epigenetics play in evolution — can acquired traits be inherited more than we thought?
